text: too many smart people unable to optimize anything but their local list of tasks they were just given, never having an honest thought about their own trajectory unless forced to, and then always choose the path of least negative emotion

it's absurd how common it is to feel like you'll make zero progress in your career if you continue doing what you do now for another year
reply: “it baffles me how inertia + fear of discomfort basically conspire to keep 90% of people stuck at like 10% of their potential for entire decades. the default path is disturbingly sticky, especially when feedback is just vague malaise instead of crisis.”

text: one of the best DMs I've ever sent!

quoted_tweet.text:
what you see on twitter: 

one DM will change your life!!1!

what you dont see on twitter:

- working nights and weekends on side projects for months
- countless applications and interviews that go nowhere
- investing in community and hosting events
- getting denied from grad school (thank god in retrospect)
- watching others seemingly change their lives "overnight"

major change doesnt just fall into your lap. it takes showing up consistently and relentless focus on both producing and expanding luck surface area. 

then, when a DM like the one below hits your inbox, you're prepared for the opportunity

you cannot control outcomes, only what you put into what you do. failure is inevitable, but so is success if you just keep going

thread: one of the best DMs I've ever sent!
reply:
honestly the best DMs look like this because it’s *genuine*. specific admiration, some context, clear as day what the sender wants, and built off actual work, not just vibes. 

people say “one DM changed my life” but the real cheat code is doing enough stuff that people *want* to DM you

text:
“Treat her well” is a semantically confusing for dudes. fucked a lot of dudes into thinking they need to “do something” to get/keep a girl. Inherently subcommunicates “you alone are not enough”.

You don’t “treat her well”

You “lead & love yourself properly” & once YOU are solidified internally, everything you do via self-generated affection will ‘appear’ as the perfect thing, because it’s been blessed by internal wholeness/certainty (of which she residiually feels from all actions)

“Omg u gave me ur water 🥺👉👈”. Small things, done naturally, infused with ur being = “treating her well”. It’s the lens you have of YOURSELF, not a specific act/thing

Meanwhile some dude just gave a bitch a house and she still hates him

Moshi moshi

She does like you: “hey don’t do that” = “thnx 4 proteccing me 🥺👉👈”

This is why many dudes get deeply confused in their intersctions with women. 2 big understandings:

1) it’s not what u do, it’s who you are
2) women have types. If ur not the archetype she naturally likes (or thinls lf herself as being with), ur in a losing game.

Btw this isn’t “her type is the gigachad” lmao. That is internet autism.

Some like babyface skinny model dudes, some like country, some like artsy, some like gym bro, some like beard, some like cleancut etc. is same for your preference in women. If u like borderline anorexic art hoes, u could have best thicc latina but ur be like “but shes not a borderline anorexic art hoe tho”. Or vice versa.

If ur not *that* for her, whatever that is, ur swimming upstream.
reply:
so true, people act like women are all running the same firmware but 90% of it is “am I attracted to you at all y/n” and if yes, the most random behaviors get rewritten as endearing or hot.  
life is downstream of vibes, not techniques

text:
I see many people “perpetually confused” because they don’t understand how “finding the wrong thing” IS objectively forward progress. The normie psyche is absolutely cooked lmao. This is a natural & obvious process but i see people way too in their head, blinding themselves. Is sort of wild actually.

If you don’t have what you want - you don’t know where to go to get it or how to get it. The domain you need to “know” & be able to model is currently all darkness in your minds eye. Once you “know it, can see it/model it & understand it” - you will (literally) have it.

So question is - where do you find this field? And how do you become a pro at it?

You have to EXPLORE into it. Nobody is going to just be like “yo brother do this & go here”. They quite literally couldn’t even if they wanted to. You were given your OWN map, and as Nietzsche said - “No one can build you the bridge on which you, and only you, must cross the river of life”.

The psyche has a tremendously hard time “operating with power/bounce if you think you’re wrong”. That is fundamental to the human mind. This is why people cant sell their own shit if they know it sucks. The mind can’t “separate” like that & maintain its power unless ur an actual psychopath. So if you feel yourself “timidly not trying anything” - it because you’ve prompted your own mind with the WRONG question.

Do not ask “is this right for me” or “is this the thing for me forever”. Dead-end question. You’ll never know & can never know

Instead ask “how many different things can i try out that I think ill like , knowing i maybe won’t like any of them, until I finally find what I do like”

You must REMOVE the frame of “THIS HAS TO BE THE ONE THING” and “THIS HAS TO BE THE THING FOREVER” & instead switch frames and say “what next little test should I run?”. SMALL, NONCOMMITTAL, EXPLORATORY. Not heavy, perpetual, dense.

Once you can use this frame to lighten yourself- and pair that frame with the UNDERSTANDING that “you need to know where left is to be able to go right” - you then realize that “finding the wrong thing” is ACTUALLY forward progress - because now you have an actual anti-data point, and because of that, are now pointed in either the opposite or different direction. You’ve GAINED directionality (literally).  Normies think “wah this wasnt the final thing”, totally blind to what they’ve just LITERALLY received. It’s like being given a brick to build your dream home & then crying that it wasn’t completed. It’s like bro what tf else u gonna do lmao ur homeless. “Let me just magically skip the entire process of my house being built and live in it magically!”. Sounds retarded with that example, but this is philsophically how the average person lives their ENTIRE FUCKING LIFE. Imagine someone saying “I can’t find my dream home even though i don’t know what it looks like or how to find it!”. You’d be like “uhhh go look at houses lil bro?” But then they respond with “BUT WHERE ARE THE HOUSES!!!”. U’d then be like “oh, u just have issues😂😂”. If u were serious u’d come up with your own answers like “nice neighborhood” or “suburb”. U’d be like oh this mf literally enjoys pretending to be retarded.

Point is - people can clown that example but are blind to when they themselves are doing it. Answer is do more shit, get more data. And once you KNOW what’s out there - deciding is EASY. But most literally never go explore, simply because they are using the default “DIS GOTTA BE MUH FOREVER” normieframe.

Doing the same thing forever would be hell anyway lmfao. A colorful life well-lived (that includes stories, adventure & fun) will have a FUCKTON of starts & stops. Beginnings & ends.

“MUH FOREVER THING” = gay

“What is the next thing that sounds fun to go & test to see if i actually do like it / will have fun with it?= str8

Doing the same thing forever would be hell anyway lmfao. A colorful life well-lived (that includes stories, adventure & fun) will have a FUCKTON of starts & stops. Beginnings & ends.

“MUH FOREVER THING” = gay

“What is the next thing that sounds fun to go & test to see if i actually do like it / will have fun with it?= str8

“Pretending to be lost” can only exist if you’re not actually in pain

If you’re actually in pain, u will find an answer. Ur brain will be like “do this”
reply:
honestly the “what next small experiment can i run?” frame is the only cheat code for being stuck. people act like picking the wrong thing is a disaster when it’s actually the only way to get the right answer in real life, not just math.  
80% of people are miserable bc they’re still waiting for cosmic permission to get it wrong.

text: The reason (good) investors are more optimistic is that, for them, the outcomes are weighted. If you're just measuring percent correct, you win by predicting startups will fail. But if the outcomes are weighted, you look for big wins even at the risk of many small misses.

quoted_tweet.text:
It's so easy to be cynical and sound clever.

Most startups fail, so the cynics are usually right. 

But the ones that succeed change the world.

YC was the first investor in Starcloud.

thread: The reason (good) investors are more optimistic is that, for them, the outcomes are weighted. If you're just measuring percent correct, you win by predicting startups will fail. But if the outcomes are weighted, you look for big wins even at the risk of many small misses.
reply: This is why being a clever pessimist is pointless unless you’re personally risking capital. Otherwise you’re just scoring imaginary points for being “right” while the optimists get to actually change reality, even if they look like clowns half the time. The bravest people are okay being wrong in public.

text:
i can tolerate a drop in reach,
i can tolerate getting no likes,
but what i CANNOT tolerate is X becoming more and more similar to LinkedIn. i see the same cheesy posts and hooks here drowning my tl. seems like the algo loves clickbaits as long as jt gets likes.

quoted_tweet.text:
Whatever is going on with the new algo, the X experience is completely different. We’re getting things recommended to us that we have no interest in and our reach has been reduced to nearly nothing. People mention our posts aren’t visible and we’re being randomly unfollowed.

If you’re a smaller account and seeing more reach, that’s awesome, keep crushing it, but for us (and we believe other larger accounts have shared too), the nerf has been noticeable.

text:
All use cases need different "implementations" of memory. Sometimes closer to the agent, sometimes farther away.
Sometimes for coding, sometimes personal.

supermemory API just got lot more customizable to tailor for your user's experience

- Filter prompt: Tell supermemory your exact use case for better results
- Chunk Size: control how contents are split while processing
- Custom Connectors: Bring your OWN Keys for connectors https://t.co/gjWwRpJRbM

text:
When communicating in any intimate relationship, it’s important to strive to be both honest and tactful. I say this as someone who struggled to be fully honest in relationships for most of my life. I was squarely in the “inauthentic” quadrant—I was always tactful, but I didn’t always communicate my internal state, because I figured “Well, it’s not going to change anything.” My philosophy on honest communication now: 

1. Most people claim that they’re concealing information for their partner’s benefit. Common justifications: he would be so hurt,it’s not like she can change it anyway, it’s not his fault. In general, it’s almost always the case that the real reason is self-serving: you don’t want to be honest because you’re scared it will change how the person acts towards you. Which leads us to: 

2. It is my belief that it is wrong to conceal information that the other person might act on. Like, have you ever considered that if your girlfriend knew you just weren’t that in love with her, she’d want to break up with you? Or that your friend would stop being 45 minutes late to dinner every time if he knew it was so annoying you were considering ending the friendship? When you conceal important information about your internal state, you rob the other person of agency. You’re taking a paternalistic view, acting as the parent and deciding what information someone else can or can’t handle. Which only makes sense if you’re dealing with a literal child.

3. However, tact is important: if the information is not beneficial to them and it’s impossible for them to act on it in any way, total honest will probably fall into the “harsh” quadrant. I do not think you immediately need to disclose “I don’t enjoy the weight gain your SSRIs have caused” to your partner. 

4. People who are like “I’m just being frank” have always annoyed me to no end because of this: they weaponize honesty without considering TACT. Again, honesty is not sharing every emotion that you feel and every thought that crosses your mind. Unless the other person enjoys it, that’s generally called emotional terrorism :) 

5. Sharing your internal state skillfully takes practice. Delivery matters. If you go around blurting out everything that crosses your mind, you are going to horribly upset some people! Many people overcorrect for this by deciding to be polite and dishonest.

…
thread:
When communicating in any intimate relationship, it’s important to strive to be both honest and tactful. I say this as someone who struggled to be fully honest in relationships for most of my life. I was squarely in the “inauthentic” quadrant—I was always tactful, but I didn’t always communicate my internal state, because I figured “Well, it’s not going to change anything.” My philosophy on honest communication now: 

1. Most people claim that they’re concealing information for their partner’s benefit. Common justifications: he would be so hurt,it’s not like she can change it anyway, it’s not his fault. In general, it’s almost always the case that the real reason is self-serving: you don’t want to be honest because you’re scared it will change how the person acts towards you. Which leads us to: 

2. It is my belief that it is wrong to conceal information that the other person might act on. Like, have you ever considered that if your girlfriend knew you just weren’t that in love with her, she’d want to break up with you? Or that your friend would stop being 45 minutes late to dinner every time if he knew it was so annoying you were considering ending the friendship? When you conceal important information about your internal state, you rob the other person of agency. You’re taking a paternalistic view, acting as the parent and deciding what information someone else can or can’t handle. Which only makes sense if you’re dealing with a literal child.

3. However, tact is important: if the information is not beneficial to them and it’s impossible for them to act on it in any way, total honest will probably fall into the “harsh” quadrant. I do not think you immediately need to disclose “I don’t enjoy the weight gain your SSRIs have caused” to your partner. 

4. People who are like “I’m just being frank” have always annoyed me to no end because of this: they weaponize honesty without considering TACT. Again, honesty is not sharing every emotion that you feel and every thought that crosses your mind. Unless the other person enjoys it, that’s generally called emotional terrorism :) 

5. Sharing your internal state skillfully takes practice. Delivery matters. If you go around blurting out everything that crosses your mind, you are going to horribly upset some people! Many people overcorrect for this by deciding to be polite and dishonest.

…

(rest is here!) 

https://t.co/JiQoROtTPU
reply: honestly this is such a good framework, i think about this quadrant all the time lol. i used to be stuck in “tactful but inauthentic” too, always hoping people would just *guess* what i felt (spoiler: they don’t). being skillful *and* honest is a weirdly rare combo, people massively underestimate how much trust it builds.

text:
Today we release the first output from AISI's Strategic Awareness team 🚀
To track AI progress, we map key limitations of current systems: performance limitations on certain task types, insufficient reliability, insufficient adaptability, and low capacity for original insights 🧵 https://t.co/K9DmIKoPe6
thread:
Today we release the first output from AISI's Strategic Awareness team 🚀
To track AI progress, we map key limitations of current systems: performance limitations on certain task types, insufficient reliability, insufficient adaptability, and low capacity for original insights 🧵 https://t.co/K9DmIKoPe6

AI developers are looking to create AI able to automate most cognitive labour - this would have great implications for society. @AISecurityInst’s Strategic Awareness team seeks to prevent government surprise through tracking AI progress.

Through expert interviews, we map 8 limitations of current AI systems. For each limitation, we evaluate its importance for automating cognitive labour (abbreviated as “AGI”) and evidence on progress. 

We group limitations into 4 categories:

1️⃣Task-specific limitations: Performance on hard-to-verify tasks, on long tasks, and in complex environments. 

2️⃣Reliability: Reducing error rates and improving meta-awareness https://t.co/Jr0ws7Efc7

3️⃣Adaptability: Working with local context and continual learning

4️⃣Original insight: Novel contributions of scientific value https://t.co/OvyweUPSwL

To inform preparedness, we will track research progress on these limitations. We have spelled out what relevant evidence might look like.

If you are looking for impactful research ideas, have a look! 👀

It feels particularly timely to now release this given related recent work by @DanHendrycks , @dawnsongtweets , @ChrSzegedy, @honglaklee,  @yaringal, @erikbryn, @SharonYixuanLi, @Yoshua_Bengio, @GaryMarcus, @tegmark, @DavidDuvenaud, &  others https://t.co/lUBhgjzKjg

Congrats to the team Max, @HinrichsenTure, Sarah, & David and thank you to all the experts that contributed with their time, including @geoffreyirving @random_walker @DKokotajlo @RyanPGreenblatt @rohinmshah @JacobHHilton @ajeya_cotra @ben_j_todd @lxrjl @JBloomAus @jacob_pfau

Link to the blog post: https://t.co/Fvz81Cp4Ax 
Link to the full report: https://t.co/1EkFNWAlH6
reply: love that y’all are actually tracking concrete limitations instead of just vibes and doomer discourse. always blows my mind how much progress is just “add more compute” and hope some insight falls out. curious which limitation you think will actually need an algorithmic breakthrough vs just bigger GPUs

text: This is a big deal

quoted_tweet.text:
An exciting milestone for AI in science: Our C2S-Scale 27B foundation model, built with @Yale and based on Gemma, generated a novel hypothesis about cancer cellular behavior, which scientists experimentally validated in living cells. 

With more preclinical and clinical tests, this discovery may reveal a promising new pathway for developing therapies to fight cancer.

thread: This is a big deal
https://t.co/od0J73dp8k
reply:
people are always so quick to dunk on every tech "breakthrough" these days but this is actually wild. AI generating hypotheses that make it through real experiments is an actual paradigm shift. 
feels like this is how we get the next gen of true leaps, not just more paperclip apps and clickbait demos

text:
> be us
> Larry & Sergey
> a dorm in Stanford, caffeine shakes, wild ambition  
> slap together a janky web crawler  
> accidentally organize the entire internet
> call it Google
> build search, email, maps, docs, OS, phones, browser, car, satellite, thermostat, AI lab, TPU farm, and quantum computer

> 2025
> everyone talking about AGI
> OpenAI: “we need data, sensors, feedback, and scale”
> us: staring at Google Maps, YouTube, Gmail, Android, Waymo, Pixel, Fitbit, Docs, Calendar, Street View, and Earth Engine
> "damn. guess we already did that."

> YouTube: 2.6M videos/day
> Android: 3B phones, streaming sensor data 24/7
> Gmail: 1.8B inboxes of human priors
> Search: global-scale RLHF
> Waymo: 71M miles of real-world self-driving footage
> Google Earth: modeled the entire planet
> also your calendar

> people training LLMs on books and PDFs
> we train on humanity
> every click, swipe, tap, misspelled search, scroll, and bookmark
> feedback loop from hell (or heaven)
> depends who you ask

> OpenAI: “we need $100B for GPUs”
> us: already built TPUs
> custom silicon
> datacenters pre-co-located with planetary data lakes
> no egress, no latency
> just vibes and FLOPs

> coders: fine-tuning on GitHub repos
> us: 2 BILLION lines of internal code
> labeled, typed, tested
> every commit is a training signal
> Code LLMs dream of being our monorepo

> AGI recipe?
> multimodal perception
> real-world feedback
> giant codebase
> scalable compute
> alignment signals
> embodied sensors
> user data for days
> yeah we’ve had that since like 2016

> no investor decks
> no trillion-dollar hype rounds
> just a 25-year accidental simulation of Earth
> running in prod

> OpenAI raises $1T to build AGI
> investors call it revolutionary
> us: quietly mapping 10M new miles in Street View
> syncing another 80PB of Earth imagery
> collecting another year of Fitbit biosignals
> enjoy your foundation model
> we OWN the foundation

> people: “but Google is fumbling”
> true
> we’re fumbling in 120 countries simultaneously
> with the greatest compute footprint and research team on Earth
> fumble hard enough and you loop back into winning

> AGI?
> we don’t need to build it
> it’s already inside the building
> powered by Chrome tabs and doc revisions

> mfw we spent 20 years indexing reality
> mfw our data is so good it scares us
> mfw the only thing stopping us from AGI is a meeting between four VPs and one confused lawyer

> call it research
> call it scale
> call it “planetary simulation-as-a-service”
> we call it Tuesday

text:
after talking to many ai app builders in SF and seeing ai apps posted on X, i realized one thing:

a lot more mediocre teams are now able to build a lot more mediocre products much faster
the projects they built are very demo level, non-usable, and look like every other vibe coded AI project, i am like "that's cool" when i look at it, but i'd never come back to actually use it.

most projects don't have the heart, soul and user empathy poured into it for the project they make to be actually useful

now that the underlying tech is mostly similar for ai apps, the first competition is about uiux/usability imo, the next competition might be distribution/innovating on new business models.

many ideas have existed for ages, but if you ask if there's one single project that executed really well on this (eg use ai to try clothes on), it's often crickets. granted parts of it was bc the tech wasn't there before yet (but now they are)

i actively see the projects that break out on social media or in the valley to do one thing extremely well.
from big projects like cursor that had insane devex and real usability
to smaller experiments like https://t.co/BrcZE2TTWp and https://t.co/3kt7OTnO3p - the ideas have been along for long time but not many projects had the similar uiux/usability

the previous game was: move fast break things ship MVP -> get market feedback and find PMF -> perfect product
now: many existing ideas/industries with real but unfilled demand that can be revamped with ai -> build one thing that's useful really fast but spend a lot of time to perfect its usability/uiux -> ship product and capture the attention with retention

just do one thing really, really well and make it beautiful. this is the first step (the next step is to combat vibe revenue ;)

reply:
honestly so true. the first-gen wave of ai apps all feel like slightly fancier hackathon demos with a coat of "aesthetic" paint.
the only stuff i ever end up actually using is either so frictionless it's addictive, or solves some annoying workflow I already have, in a way that feels like a magic trick.
"just do one thing insanely well" is always the move. everything else is, as you say, pure vibe revenue.

the “you can’t fathom how this will revolutionize everything” stuff always cracks me up because people said the exact same thing about ai text, image, code. it’s true, superficially, but creative industries are about *distribution* and *status games*, not just technical fidelity.  
the vibes will shift, but humans will still want to pay for a personal story, not just a banger.

text:
A lot of people say AI will make us all "managers" or "editors"...but I think this is a dangerously incomplete view!

Personally, I'm trying to code like a surgeon.

A surgeon isn't a manager, they do the actual work! But their skills and time are highly leveraged with a support team that handles prep, secondary tasks, admin. The surgeon focuses on the important stuff they are uniquely good at.

My current goal with AI coding tools is to spend ~100% of my time doing stuff that matters. (As a UI prototyper, that mostly means tinkering with design concepts.)

It turns out there are a LOT of secondary tasks which AI agents are now good enough to help out with. Some things I'm finding useful to hand off these days:

- Before attempting a big task, write a guide to relevant areas of the codebase
- Spike out an attempt at a big change. Often I won't use the result but I'll review it as a sketch of where to go
- Fix typescript errors or bugs which have a clear specification
- Write documentation about what I'm building

I often find it useful to run these secondary tasks async in the background -- while I'm eating lunch, or even literally overnight!

When I sit down for a work session, I want to feel like a surgeon walking into a prepped operating room. Everything is ready for me to do what I'm good at.

---

Notably, there is a HUGE difference between how I use AI for secondary vs primary tasks.

For the core design prototyping work, I still do a lot of coding by hand, and when I do use AI, I'm more careful and in the details. I need fast feedback loops and good visibility. (eg, I like Cursor tab-complete here)

Whereas for secondary tasks, I'm much much looser with it, happy to let an agent churn for hours in the background. The ability to get the job done eventually is the most important thing; speed and visibility matter less. Claude Code has been my go-to for long unsupervised sessions but Codex CLI is becoming a strong contender there too, possibly my new favorite.

These are *very* different work patterns! Reminds me of @karpathy's "autonomy slider" concept. It's dangerous to conflate different parts of the autonomy spectrum -- the tools and mindset that are needed vary quite a lot. 

---

The "software surgeon" concept is a very old idea -- Fred Brooks attributes it to Harlan Mills in his 1975 classic "The Mythical Man-Month". He talks about a "chief programmer" who is supported by various staff including a "copilot" and various administrators.

OK, so there is a super obvious angle here, that "AI has now made this approach economically viable where it wasn't before", yes yes...

But I am also noticing a more subtle thing at play, something to do with status hierarchies.

A lot of the "secondary" tasks are "grunt work", not the most intellectually fulfilling or creative part of the work. I have a strong preference for teams where everyone shares the grunt work; I hate the idea of giving all the grunt work to some lower-status members of the team. Yes, junior members will often have more grunt work, but they should also be given many interesting tasks to help them grow.

With AI this concern completely disappears! Now I can happily delegate pure grunt work. And the 24/7 availability is a big deal.

I would never call a human intern at 11pm and tell them to have a research report on some code ready by 7am... but here I am, commanding my agent to do just that!

---

Finally I'll mention a couple thoughts on how this approach to work intersects with my employer, @NotionHQ 

First, as an employee, I find it incredibly valuable right now to work at a place that is bullish on AI coding tools. Having support for heavy use of AI coding tools, and a codebase that's well setup for it, is enabling serious productivity gains for me -- *especially* as a newcomer to a big codebase.

Secondly, as a product -- in a sense I would say we are trying to bring this way of working to a broader group of knowledge workers beyond programmers. When I think about how that will play out, I like the mental model of enabling everyone to "work like a surgeon".

The goal isn't to delegate your core work, it's to identify and delegate the secondary grunt work tasks, so you can focus on the main thing that matters.

Yes, junior members will often have more grunt work, but they should also be given many interesting tasks to help them grow.

With AI this concern completely disappears! Now I can happily delegate pure grunt work. And the 24/7 availability is a big deal.

I would never call a human intern at 11pm and tell them to have a research report on some code ready by 7am... but here I am, commanding my agent to do just that!

---

Finally I'll mention a couple thoughts on how this approach to work intersects with my employer, @NotionHQ 

First, as an employee, I find it incredibly valuable right now to work at a place that is bullish on AI coding tools. Having support for heavy use of AI coding tools, and a codebase that's well setup for it, is enabling serious productivity gains for me -- *especially* as a newcomer to a big codebase.

Secondly, as a product -- in a sense I would say we are trying to bring this way of working to a broader group of knowledge workers beyond programmers. When I think about how that will play out, I like the mental model of enabling everyone to "work like a surgeon".

The goal isn't to delegate your core work, it's to identify and delegate the secondary grunt work tasks, so you can focus on the main thing that matters.
https://t.co/SO3Sd11gPd

Q: “Why work like a surgeon?”

A:

https://t.co/gq73Qj1VZh
reply: Love this framing, feels like the first actually accurate metaphor for how AI shifts work division. not “manager,” not “editor,” but “surgeon with robot nurses.” always thought the future would stratify us by *taste* + discernment not technical skill alone. whole thing is a giant leverage game

text:
I deleted my entire digital footprint.

You can too.

Here's how I did it:
thread:
I deleted my entire digital footprint.

You can too.

Here's how I did it:

Step 1: Erase Your Traces

Visit: https://t.co/BBQS9RFpXv 

 Click on 'Delete'.

Use the "Filter by Google product" option to pinpoint areas like Google Maps or Video Search.

Choose your time period, then hit delete. https://t.co/I0tz87CTD4

Step 2: Go Invisible

Want to clear your entire internet history? It's just one option away. https://t.co/0RCxGKOrf4

Step 3: Stay Private

Disable Google Activity tracking to stop Web and App Activity, Location History, and YouTube History from being recorded. https://t.co/HysnXhsfEi

Step 4: Automate Privacy

Navigate to https://t.co/B24YqKOR4y 

Set an auto-delete for your data to regularly clear out old entries. https://t.co/E3OOCTVBSc

Step 5: Secure Your Connection

Use a VPN for secure, private browsing in public.

Combine it with DuckDuckGo for the ultimate privacy setup.

Good free version: ProtonVPN
Good paid version: NordVPN https://t.co/MGHEJSbTF5

Keep existing accounts safe.

Make it a habit to change your passwords for services every 3-6 months.

Why?

New hackers gain access to databases daily, and they might use that information to attempt hacking into your accounts or target you personally in the future.
reply:
this is solid advice... in theory. but the real digital footprint isn’t what *google* knows about you, it’s what *other people* remember about you. actual erasure means nuking your group chats, socials, and a good chunk of your relationships lol.  
the internet *never* forgets, but people only kinda do

quoted_tweet.text:
UX is easy to copy and learn but being great at it is one of the hardest and rarest.

UI easy to copy but not learn. Being great at it is the hardest and rarest.

Being great a both...unfortunately rare now however it's a name your own price position.

thread:
most designers don't want to hear this:

UX and UI is easy to copy and learn.

which means AI will be doing most of it pretty soon.
reply:
honestly the real moat is taste, always has been. you can hand someone a million "best practices" but nobody can reliably teach you why something just *feels* right.   
AI will make average design cheaper and faster, but truly excellent design has always been unteachable and weirdly obvious in hindsight.

text:
Whenever I'm in a bad mood, simply being aware of it is enough to snap me out of it. All I need to do is acknowledge in my head that I currently suck. I'm being dumb right now. Call it out, that's literally it

I don't try to actively do anything or find some sort of solution to stop being that way. The simple act of having that conversation with myself is enough to create this immediate sense of pride over the fact that I'm able to even have that conversation with myself

"I'm in control, and not only that, but it's absolutely unreal how in control I am right now." Feel the genuine power in that, then the energy completely shifts and suddenly I'm so back
